segments.s395-g97: تنش is تن (body) + the enclitic ش marking third‑person possession: “his body.” گشت is the past of گشتن used here in a causative/becoming sense (“became/grew”), so گشت لرزان = “became trembling.” رخساره زرد is a noun + adjective (complexion/face + pale) describing his looks; the coordinating و simply links the two clauses.
segments.s395-g98: همی is an archaic/poetic particle indicating continuous or ongoing aspect (“kept on” / “still”). رفت is the simple past of رفتن used here as a motion verb (“went/moved”); پویان is a participial/adjectival form meaning “moving/active,” so همی رفت پویان = “he kept moving about.” دل پر ز درد is an idiomatic phrase: دل (heart) + پر (full of) + ز (poetic ‘of/from’) + درد (pain/sorrow) = “heart full of pain.”
segments.s395-g99: سراپرده (inner curtain/curtain before the private chamber) is the object; زد is the simple past of زدن here meaning “struck” or “struck aside.” In Persian poetry the verb may precede the subject, so شهریار جوان (the young king) comes after the verb as the agent: “the young king struck the curtain.”
segments.s395-g100: به + گردش here indicates motion/entry into a circle or gathering (به = toward/into). گردش literally means a circuit/round and functions as ‘circle’ or ‘assembly’ in this context. دلیران روشن‌روان is a compound description of the men: دلیران (the brave ones) modified by روشن‌روان (literally “bright‑souled,” i.e. cheerful/spirited).
segments.s395-g101: زمین را uses را as the definite/direct‑object marker: “the ground.” به دیبا here functions instrumentally — “with brocade” — and دیبا is brocade/silk cloth. بیاراستند is the past plural of بیاراستن (بیاراست + ـند), meaning “they adorned/ornamented,” so the clause means “they adorned the ground with brocade.”
segments.s395-g102: نشستند is the past plural meaning “they sat down” (they took their seats). بر خوان = “at/on the table/banquet” (بر = on/upon, خوان = banquet/table). و می‌خواستند is an unfinished clause meaning “and they wanted/were about to” (می‌خواستند = they intended/were preparing to), leaving the action implicit. Note: one provided sense list for نشستند did not match the actual meaning here, so the sense index for that word is set to -1.
segments.s395-g25: اگر introduces a condition ('if'). پیل is the subject (elephant). بیند is a present/optative verb here ('were to see' / 'sees') used in classical poetic narrative. برآرد is the result verb ('will lift / will bring up'); the clause has two verbs where the first (بیند) sets the condition and the second (برآرد) gives the outcome, and به + ابر marks the goal ('into/to the clouds').
segments.s395-g26: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. دریا specifies the source ('from the sea'). نهنگ is the object moved ('a whale'). و coordinates the second object phrase. به خشکی marks the land-side counterpart ('to / on the land'), and هژبر is a proper name or epithet placed as the thing taken from the land.
segments.s395-g27: نبیند here is a negated verbal form meaning 'he does not/ will not experience' (classical usage where 'see' = 'experience'). ز برداشتن marks what is being experienced / done ('from lifting'); هیچ رنج is the negated noun phrase 'any hardship'. The whole clause: 'he experiences no hardship from lifting.'
segments.s395-g28: تو is the (poetic) topical/agentive 'you'; او را is the marked object ('him'). چو ... و چو introduces two similes ('as/like a wolf and as/like a sorcerer'). مسنج is an archaic/poetic form related to 'سنجیدن' (to measure / judge); here it functions as the verb-like instruction or descriptor meaning 'weigh/measure/assess him' (imperative or descriptive participial use).
segments.s395-g29: دو بچه است is an existential construction ('there are two young ones' / 'two young accompany him'); با او indicates accompaniment ('with him'); به بالای او specifies position ('on/atop him' — 'on his back / above him'). The copula است follows the numeral as in classical ordering.
segments.s395-g30: همان رای means 'the same counsel/opinion/plan'; پیوسته modifies it ('continually' / 'constantly'). با رای او ('with his counsel') shows that this opinion is joined or in agreement with his own plan — repeated رای is a stylistic emphasis on agreement.
segments.s395-g31: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). او is the subject ('he'). بر هوا رفت is a verbal phrase 'went up into the air / rose'. و connects the two coordinated actions; گسترد is the verb 'spread' and پر is its object ('wing' or 'feathers'). Word order follows classical Persian: temporal marker + subject + verb(s) + object.
segments.s395-g32: ندارد is the negative possession/attribution verb ('does not have' / 'has no'). It governs a coordination: زمین هوش و خورشید فر — 'the earth (has) no awareness and the sun (has) no splendour.' In Persian the single verb can apply to both coordinated nouns. The nouns following ندارد are objects/attributes lacking.
segments.s395-g65: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a past-time clause. دیدند is 3rd-person plural past 'they saw'; سیمرغ را is the definite object marked by را; بچگان (literally 'the little ones') is the grammatical subject placed after the verb and object for poetic emphasis (V‑O‑S order).
segments.s395-g66: خروشان is a participial/adjectival form 'roaring/tumultuous' describing the same subject; و simply links the following clause. خون از دو دیده چکان is a small clause where خون ('blood') is the theme, از دو دیده ('from both eyes') is the source phrase, and چکان here is a verb 'dripped' — the subject is understood from the previous segment (the young ones).
segments.s395-g67: چنان is an adverb 'so/thus' signalling degree or result. بردمیدند is an archaic/poetic past plural meaning 'they carried off/took away' (bardamīdan-type formation). از آن جایگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from that place' giving the source of the action; the subject is again implied from context.
segments.s395-g68: که introduces a result clause 'so that/that'. از سهمشان literally 'from their share' (here idiomatically 'of their number' or 'deprived of their portion'). دیده is used as 'eye/vision' (nominative); گم کرد is the past tense of 'گم کردن' ('to lose' — گم + کرد, with کرد functioning as the main past verb here); راه is the direct object 'way/path' — together 'the eye lost the way' i.e. 'lost its bearings'.
segments.s395-g69: چو 'when' opens a temporal clause. سیمرغ is the subject. زان تیغها ('from/ by those blades') uses زان (poetic 'from that') to indicate cause/means. گشت is a past/telic verb 'became/turned', and سست is the predicative adjective 'weak' — 'became weak because of those swords.'
segments.s395-g70: به here indicates manner/means ('with/ in'), خوناب is a compound meaning 'blood‑water/stream of blood', and صندوق و گردون are two coordinated objects 'the chest (box) and the sky/heaven' (poetic hyperbole). بشست is 3rd‑person singular past 'washed' — 'he/she/it washed (them) in blood.'
segments.s395-g71: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. صندوق بیرون شد is a verb phrase 'came out of the chest/box' (بیرون شد = 'went out/ emerged'); شد functions as the past auxiliary/main verb here. The proper name اسفندیار follows the verb (V‑S order common in epic poetry) and is the agent who emerges.
segments.s395-g72: بغرید is past 3rd‑person singular 'he bellowed/roared'. با introduces an instrumental phrase 'with/by'; آلت کارزار is a compound 'the instrument(s) of battle' — i.e. weapons/implements of war — specifying how he appeared or acted.
segments.s395-g49: همی + past verb is an archaic/perfective-progressive construction: همی رفت means 'he kept going' or 'he rode on (swiftly)'. چون باد is a simile 'like the wind' placed after the verb; فرمانروا (the subject) follows the verb here (verb–subject order is common in epic Persian), so the clause reads 'the sovereign rode like the wind.'
segments.s395-g50: یکی کوه = 'a certain mountain' (یکی used as an indefinite determiner before the noun). دیدش is the past tense 'he saw' with the clitic -ش referring back to that mountain (object enclitic). سر اندر هوا is a descriptive phrase 'its head/summit in the air' (سر = summit/top; اندر + هوا = 'in the air'), giving the mountain's appearance.
segments.s395-g51: بر آن سایه = 'that shadow' with بر (on/upon) marking placement; the repeated بر before اسپ و گردون emphasizes 'upon the horse and (upon) the sky/heavens'. بداشت here functions as a past verbal action 'he placed/put/kept' (classical usage of داشتن/بداشتن) meaning he caused the shadow to fall upon those things; word order puts the locative/goal phrases immediately after the shadow.
segments.s395-g52: روان را uses the object marker را to mark 'his soul/spirit' as definite; به اندیشه اندر گماشت is a classical causative/putting construction: گماشت (past of گماشتن) = 'entrusted/placed', with به اندیشه اندر = 'into thought' (اندر intensifies the locative 'in'), so the whole clause means 'he committed his spirit to thought' or 'he set his mind to thinking.'
segments.s395-g53: همی آفرین خواند is another iterative/perfective phrase: همی + past خواند 'he kept uttering/singing'. آفرین here is an exclamation/noun 'praise' or 'acclamation' and خواند (past of خواندن) means 'uttered / sang', so the phrase means 'he repeatedly uttered praise to/for'. بر یک خدای uses بر + noun to indicate the addressee/object of praise: 'to the One God.'
segments.s395-g54: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause 'that...'. گیتی به فرمان او شد is literal 'the world by his command became' — شد is past 'became/happened' and here means 'came to be/obeyed'. به پای is an idiomatic locution ('at the foot' = 'subject/at his feet'), so the clause conveys 'that the world came under his command / stood at his feet.'
segments.s395-g55: چو as a temporal conjunction here means 'when'. سیمرغ is the subject; از دور is an adverbial phrase 'from afar / at a distance'. صندوق is the direct object and دید is the past verb 'saw' placed at the end in typical classical order: 'When the Simurgh from afar saw the chest...'.
segments.s395-g56: پسش 'behind it' (پس + -ش) indicates location relative to the chest; لشکر و ناله بوق is a coordinated object: 'an army and the lament/cry (ناله) of trumpets (بوق)'. دید again is the past verb 'saw'. The clause therefore reads 'it saw behind it an army and the sound/cry of trumpets.'
segments.s395-g89: زمین کوه تا کوه (literally “ground mountain to mountain”) is a poetic word order meaning “the ground from one mountain to another / across the mountains.” تا here marks the extent “as far as / to.” پر پر بود is a reduplicated sequence where the first پر is the noun “feather” and the second پر functions as ‘full (of)’; بود is the past copula “was.”
segments.s395-g90: ز is the preposition “from / because of.” پرش here is best read as a possessive/relational form (’its feathers’ or ’from its feathers’), not the listed verbal sense (so the provided senses do not match exactly). همه دشت = “the whole plain”; دشت پر فر بود = “the plain was full of فر (royal radiance/glory).” بود again is the past copula.
segments.s395-g91: بدیدند is the past tense ‘they saw.’ پر خون is an adjectival phrase ‘full/covered with blood’ (پر = full/of), modifying تن شاه را ‘the king’s body’; را is the definite/direct-object marker attaching to the whole noun phrase.
segments.s395-g92: کجا is the interrogative adverb “where/why” (rhetorical here). خیره کردی is formed from the adjectival stem خیره (’fixed, staring’) plus کرد(ی) = ‘you did’ → ‘you stared/fixed your gaze’; this yields second-person past. به introduces the object (به رخ ماه را = ‘at the moon-like face’), with را marking the definite object.
segments.s395-g93: همی is a poetic continuous/emphatic particle meaning ‘kept on/continually.’ آفرین is the acclamation ‘Bravo!’; خواندندش is ‘they called/cried (it) to him’ with the clitic -ش = ‘him.’ سران is the plural subject ‘the leaders/chiefs’ and is the group addressed by the verb.
segments.s395-g94: This is an appositive list identifying those who praised: سواران جنگی = ‘war-horsemen / military riders’ (سواران = riders, جنگی = warlike) and کنداوران = ‘excavators / diggers’ (a group name). The phrase is a nominal apposition to سران above.
segments.s395-g95: شنید is the past verb ‘he heard.’ آن سخن is the direct object ‘that speech/utterance.’ در زمان گرگسار is a temporal adjunct ‘in the time of گرگسار,’ where گرگسار is a descriptive place/time name (literally ‘wolf-infested’ — a poetic epithet for a savage time or place).
segments.s395-g96: که introduces a subordinate clause ‘that…’ پیروز شد = ‘became victorious’ (پیروز = victorious; شد = became). نامور شهریار is the predicate nominal ‘the famed/renowned ruler’ (نامور = famed, شهریار = king/lord) that the clause predicates of the subject.
segments.s395-g57: ز + noun + اندر آمد is a poetic way to say “came out of/from the mountain”: ز = 'from' (poetic), کوه is the source, اندر آمد = 'came in/ out of' (here 'came forth'). چو introduces a simile 'like/as' and ابری سیاه = 'a black cloud' (ابری = a cloud, سیاه = black/dark). The verb آمد follows the locative phrase, a common classical order.
segments.s395-g58: The paired negation نه ... نه = 'neither ... nor'. خورشید and ماه are the two items being negated; روشن is the predicate (bright/visible) that applies to them. بد and نیز are qualifiers: بد here means 'poorly/ill' (i.e. not brightly) and نیز (= 'also/likewise') intensifies the negation; the clause therefore says that neither the sun nor the moon shone/was bright.
segments.s395-g59: بدان is a demonstrative/locative phrase ('to/at that'); here it introduces 'that evil' (بد) as the topic. که introduces a subordinate clause: گردون بگیرد = 'the sky/heavens seize' (بگیرد is present/subjunctive/optative-like form used to express potential/result). به چنگ = 'with a claw' (instrument). The phrase describes the severity of the evil as something the heavens would grasp with a talon.
segments.s395-g60: بر آن سان که = 'in that manner/so that' introducing a simile; نخچیر گیرد پلنگ is a clause meaning 'a leopard seizes its prey'. Note the word order: گیرد (verb) appears before the subject پلنگ, which is a permissible poetic/classical order; the verb 'گیرد' is present/factual ('seizes').
segments.s395-g61: بر آن تیغها = 'against/on those swords' (prepositional phrase placed first for emphasis). زد is the past tense of زدن 'to strike' and here indicates that the bird's limbs/horns contacted the blades; دو پا و دو پر are the affected parts (two feet and two wings/feathers). Poetic word order places the verb before the direct objects.
segments.s395-g62: نماند is the past negative 'did not remain' (nothing was left). ایچ = 'nothing' (negation/emphatic), followed by the marked noun phrase سیمرغ را (the Simurgh, with را marking the definite object/topic). زیب و فر are the two things that were lost: زیب = beauty/adornment and فر = royal glory/splendour. The overall meaning: none of the Simurgh's beauty or glory remained.
segments.s395-g63: به چنگ و به منقار = 'with the talons and with the beak' (instruments). چندی here means 'for a while' (a temporal adverb). تپید is the past tense of تپیدن, used here for frantic beating or struggling (beating of wings/convulsive motion). The sequence describes a short period of frantic movement using claws and beak.
segments.s395-g64: چو = 'when'. تنگ اندر آمد is an idiomatic construction: تنگ = 'narrow/close', اندر آمد = 'came into' or 'was pressed into' — together meaning 'when it came into a narrow/closely confined place' or 'when it was squeezed'. فرو آرمید = فرو (down/deeply) + آرمید (rested/ceased) = 'sank down and lay still' (often euphemistic for dying). The clause describes the cessation of motion when confined.
segments.s395-g81: تو is the 2nd-person singular subject (poetic 'thou'); بردی is the simple past (2sg) of بردن here meaning 'drove/expelled'. پی + جادوان را functions as the object phrase — پی ('in pursuit of/after') marks that the action targeted the magicians (جادوان) and را is the definite-object marker attached to them. ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from (their) place' with ز = 'from' and جای = 'place'. Word order is subject – verb – object phrase – source phrase, typical of poetry.
segments.s395-g82: تو بودی is 'you were' (بودی = past of بودن). بدین here is the archaic/poetic equivalent of به این ('to this') formed as ب + دین; it introduces the target/measure of the following predicate. نیکیم (نیک + -یم) denotes 'goodness/virtue' (here 'this good/virtuous thing'), and رهنمای is the predicate noun 'guide'. Together: 'you were the guide to this goodness.'
segments.s395-g83: هم‌آنگه is a temporal adverb 'then/at that moment'. خروش آمد is an impersonal idiom 'a cry/clamor arose' (آمد = past 'came/arose'). از کرنای is an ablative phrase 'from the trumpet' or 'from the trumpet-blast' (کرنا = horn/trumpet; کرنای here indicates the sound-source). The order places the time before the event and the source after the verb, as typical.
segments.s395-g84: پشوتن is a proper name (the actor). بیاورد is the past (or narrative-present) 3rd-person singular of آوردن 'he/they brought' used here narratively. پرده‌سرای is the direct object 'curtain (of the stage) / stage-scene'; the verb directly governs it without an object marker (poetic economy). The clause means 'Peshotan brought (forth) the stage-curtain.'
segments.s395-g85: This segment lists nominal items. سلیح here is a noun 'weapon/armament' presented as the first item. برادر سپاه is a genitive phrase 'brother/comrade of the army' (برادر + noun) and و پسر begins to add another item 'and (the) son...'; the coordinating و links items in a catalogue. The nouns stand in apposition/listing rather than forming a full clause.
segments.s395-g86: بزرگان ایران is a genitive phrase 'the great ones of Iran' (بزرگان governing ایران). The following و تاج و کمر continue the list: 'and crown and belt' (تاج = crown/honor, کمر = girdle/belt). The repeated و is simple coordination in a series.
segments.s395-g87: از آن کشته is a prepositional/partitive phrase 'from that slaughter / from those slain' (کشته as noun 'the slain'). کس ... ندید is a negational idiom: کس here functions in the sense 'no one' when combined with the negative implication of ندید in this construction. روی هامون means 'the face/surface of the plain' (روی = face/surface; هامون = plain). ندید is past 'saw'. The whole gives 'no one saw the plain's surface from that slaughter.'
segments.s395-g88: جز introduces the only exception 'except/only'. اندام جنگاور is 'the bodies/forms of warriors' (اندام = body/figure; جنگاور = warrior). و خون 'and blood' completes the list of what was seen. ندید at the end repeats the verb 'saw' (negated by the earlier pattern), so the structure means 'no one saw (anything) except the warriors' bodies and blood.'
segments.s395-g1: A noun phrase giving the subject and its circumstance: جهانجوی (a person described as a 'world-seeker' or ambitious one) is followed by the prepositional phrase پیش جهان‌آفرین, where پیش means 'in the presence of/before' and جهان‌آفرین is an epithet for the Creator/God. Word order places the prepositional circumstance after the subject for poetic emphasis.
segments.s395-g2: Main predicate and adverbials. بمالید is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb ('he rubbed' / 'he anointed') with its subject implicit from the previous segment. چندی is an adverbial 'for a while'; رخ is the direct object 'face'; اندر زمین is a locative phrase 'on/into the ground'. Together they state the action performed by the subject.
segments.s395-g3: A locative clause: بر آن بیشه 'upon that grove' sets the scene; اندر سراپرده 'within the inner-curtain / inner chamber' specifies where; زد is a past 3rd-singular of زدن used poetically here ('he struck' / 'he sounded' / 'he made'), with the subject understood from context. The whole phrase establishes an action taking place in that location.
segments.s395-g4: نهادند is a past-tense 3rd-plural verb 'they placed / they set'; خوانی here denotes a banquet/table (poetic noun) that they set; چنان چون سزد is an idiomatic clause 'such as befits' where چنان 'such', چون 'as/like', and سزد is an impersonal present 3rd-singular 'it befits / it is fitting'. The clause comments that the feast was arranged appropriately.
segments.s395-g5: A brief clause giving the speaker, addressee, and time/order: به دژخیم 'to the torturer' is the indirect object, فرمود is the past 3rd-singular verb 'he commanded/said', پس is an adverb 'then', and شهریار (the subject) appears after the verb for poetic order — 'the king then commanded the torturer'.
segments.s395-g6: A subordinate clause introduced by که 'that/so that': آرند is the present-subjunctive/optative 3rd-person-plural of آوردن ('they should bring' / 'bring'), بدبخت را is the definite object 'the unfortunate one' (را marks the object), and بسته خوار are past-participle/adjectival descriptors 'bound' and 'humiliated/despised' modifying that object. The clause states the king's order.
segments.s395-g7: Simple verbal clause: ببردند is past 3rd-plural 'they led/took (him)', پیش marks the location 'before/in the presence of', and یل اسفندیار identifies the person before whom he was brought ('the hero Esfandiyar'). The implied object of ببردند is the unfortunate person referenced earlier.
segments.s395-g8: A temporal clause introduced by چو 'when': دیدار او is a noun phrase 'the sight/meeting of him' (i.e., seeing him), دید is the past verb 'saw/beheld', and پس شهریار (then the king) is the subject placed after the verb for poetic order. Literal structure: 'when the king beheld his appearance...' The sentence is left trailing into the next action.
segments.s395-g17: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' placed before the verb; داد is the past simple 'gave'; پاسخ is the verbal object 'an answer' and ورا is the enclitic/weak pronoun meaning 'to him'. The subject (گرگسار) follows the clause (a common poetic inversion), so the line literally reads 'Thus gave an answer to him the گرگسار.'
segments.s395-g18: که introduces the reported speech/continuation ('that' / 'saying'); ای (here fused as که‌ای) is a vocative marker addressing someone. پیل is the noun 'elephant', جنگی is an adjective 'warlike' placed after the noun (typical Persian order), گه is an adverb 'sometimes', and کارزار is 'battle/battlefield' — the clause is an elliptical direct address continuing the reply.
segments.s395-g19: بدین منزلت is a frozen locution meaning 'in this state/condition' (بدین = 'in this way'); here the input split it into ب + دین, but in sense it functions as an adverbial phrase. کار is the noun 'task/affair' and دشوارتر is the comparative adjective 'more difficult' modifying کار.
segments.s395-g20: گراینده‌تر is a comparative participial/adjectival form meaning 'more inclined/ resolute' (the adjective follows Persian patterns); باش is the second‑person imperative 'be!'; و is the coordinating 'and'; بیدارتر is the comparative adjective 'more awake/alert' — together an instruction: 'Be more ... and more alert.'
segments.s395-g21: یکی is 'a certain' (indefinite), کوه is 'mountain', and بینی is the verbal form 'you see' (second person singular); سر 'summit/head' is the noun modified by اندر هوا 'in the air' (اندر = 'in/within', هوا = 'air/sky') — the phrase describes seeing a mountain whose top reaches into the air.
segments.s395-g22: بر او is a prepositional phrase 'upon it' (بر = 'on/upon', او = 'it'); the repeated بر here is a poetic doubling/emphasis before یکی مرغ فرمانروا 'a certain bird, sovereign' — مرغ is 'bird' and فرمانروا is an epithet 'ruler, sovereign' placed after the noun as an apposition.
segments.s395-g23: که is a relative/introducing particle 'which/that'; سیمرغ is the mythic bird (subject); گوید here is a present/narrative form 'says/calls' (the dataset's listed senses are for the root 'گفت/گو', but the proper reading is the verb 'to say'), ورا is 'to him', and کارجوی is a compound noun 'seeker of deeds/exploits' that functions as the predicate of what the Simurgh calls him.
segments.s395-g24: چو is the comparative/simile 'like/as'; پرنده is 'bird'; کوهیست is an adjectival/predicative form 'mountain‑dwelling' or 'mountainous' attached to پرنده (i.e. 'mountain‑bird'); پیکارجوی is another compound 'seeker of battle/warrior‑seeking' — the clause says 'like a mountain bird, (he) is a seeker of battle.'
segments.s395-g73: زره در بر is an idiom meaning 'armor on' or 'wearing armor' (در + بر with nouns expresses 'having X on'). The phrase تیغ هندی به چنگ places the noun before its adjective (تیغ هندی = 'an Indian sword') and uses به with a locative/instrument sense ('into/in the claw') so that به چنگ = 'in the talon'.
segments.s395-g74: چه زور آورد is a rhetorical question/exclamation: چه here = 'what/how' and آورد (past) is used idiomatically as 'bring to bear' or 'muster' (i.e. 'what strength did [it] put forth'). مرغ پیش نهنگ places the subject (the bird) and its locative phrase 'before/in the presence of the whale' (پیش = 'in front of / before').
segments.s395-g75: همی زد is the archaic/poetic continuous past meaning 'kept striking' (همی + past verb = repeated/ongoing action). Word order here places the verb before its complements: بر او marks the target ('against him/it') and تیغ is the instrument/agentive noun ('the sword struck at him'). تا introduces the limit 'until' and پاره گشت is a resultant/passive phrase 'became torn' (پاره = 'torn', گشت = 'became').
segments.s395-g76: چنان links the result just described ('thus/so'). چاره‌گر is a noun meaning 'one who seeks/claims to provide a remedy' (agent noun) modifying مرغ ('the bird'), and بیچاره گشت is a simple past copular/resultative 'became helpless/unfortunate' (گشت = 'became').
segments.s395-g77: بیامد به پیش is a straightforward past action 'came to the presence of / came before'. خداوند ماه is a title-like nominal group 'the lord/sovereign of the Moon' (خداوند used as honorific/sovereign here, ماه = 'moon').
segments.s395-g78: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause ('for/that'), with او as the subject and داد the past-verb 'gave'. بر marks the target/recipient relationship ('to' or 'upon'), هر = 'every', and دستگاه is the object meaning 'system/apparatus/device'. The item ددی in the provided lexicon entries does not match the meaning here (likely 'demon' or 'dev'); therefore its sense is not available in the supplied senses.
segments.s395-g79: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said' — note: in the supplied senses گفت was not given as the verb 'said', so that entry does not match). کای is the archaic/vocative 'O', and داور دادگر is a stacked epithet: داور 'judge' modified by the adjective دادگر 'just'.
segments.s395-g80: خداوند here functions as an honorific/title 'lord' and is followed by a series of nouns (پاکی و زور و هنر) joined by و to list attributes: 'purity, strength, and skill/art'. The coordination simply enumerates the qualities attributed to that lord.
segments.s395-g41: سر here denotes the physical head (literally 'head') of the warriors; برگرفت is past tense (3rd sg.) of برگرفتن 'to take up/raise'. Word order is noun phrase + verb (typical Persian): 'سرِ جنگجویانِ سپه برگرفت' = 'he raised the warriors' heads (of the host)'.
segments.s395-g42: در سر گرفتن is an idiom meaning 'to take into one's head/mind' or 'to adopt (an idea)'; سخنهای سیمرغ is the object 'the Simurgh's words/sayings'. گرفت is simple past 'took/seized'. Note that سر here is idiomatic 'in the mind' (not physical head).
segments.s395-g43: همه شب = 'all night' (همه as 'whole/entire'); همی + past-verb (همی راند) is the archaic/poetic durative marker expressing continued or repeated action ('kept riding/driving'). با خود گروه = 'a group/company with him' (با + pronoun 'خود' + noun 'گروه').
segments.s395-g44: چو here = 'when'; خورشید تابان is the noun + postpositive adjective 'shining sun'; برآمد is past 'rose/appeared'; ز is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'from', followed by کوه 'mountain'.
segments.s395-g45: چراغ زمان و زمین = 'the lamp/light of time and the earth' (a poetic genitive grouping); تازه کرد is past 'made fresh/renewed' (کرد 'to make/do' with تازه 'fresh'). The verb applies to both coordinated nouns.
segments.s395-g46: در و دشت is a paired phrase 'door/town and plain' (در as 'door, gate' in the list) and بر دیگر اندازه کرد literally 'he made (them) to another measure' — بر + اندازه کردن means 'to set/put upon a measure' and دیگر indicates 'different/other'; کرد is past 'made'.
segments.s395-g47: This is a list of things taken/removed: همان اسپ و گردون و صندوق برد. همان 'that/the very', followed by nouns in a coordinated list. برد is the past verb 'took/carried' (here used in the sense 'took away' in context; note the lemma senses offered are limited but the passage uses برد in the verbal sense 'took'). Word order lists objects before the verb, as is common in Persian narrative.
segments.s395-g48: سپه را (the army as definite object marked by را) + به سالار لشکر سپرد = 'he handed/entrusted (سپرد) the army to the commander of the host.' سپرد is past tense 'to entrust/hand over'; به marks the recipient.
segments.s395-g9: The phrase places the quantified object before the verb, as standard in Persian: 'سه جام' (three cups) + a compound describing the wine 'می‌خسروانیش' and then the past verb 'داد' (gave). The compound می‌خسروانیش functions as a single nominal unit 'kingly/royal wine' with a possessive/relational ending, so the whole segment means 'he gave three goblets of royal wine.'
segments.s395-g10: This is a short result/clausal phrase with an intransitive past-form meaning 'became' (ببد) plus the epithet 'گرگسار' (the wolf-like / ferocious one) followed by an ablative 'از' + source 'می‌لعل' (ruby-coloured wine) and the adjective 'شاد' (merry). Word order places the cause ('from the ruby wine') before the adjectival result 'شاد', so: 'the ferocious one became merry from the ruby wine.'
segments.s395-g11: بدو + گفت is a clitic-dative 'to him' (بدو) plus verb 'said' (گفت) = 'he said to him.' کای is the vocative 'O', followed by ترک 'Turk' (addressed person). برگشته modifies بخت and is a past participial/adjectival phrase 'fortune/fortune has turned' (literally 'turned fortune'); the order is vocative + noun phrase describing the addressee's changed fortune.
segments.s395-g12: This is an imperative/sentence of perception: the noun phrase 'سر پیر جادو' ('the head of the old magician') appears before the verb 'ببین' (imperative 'see' or 'behold'), then the locative/ablative 'از درخت' ('on/from the tree'). The pre-verbal placement of the object followed by the imperative is a poetic, emphatic word order: 'Behold the old wizard's head on the tree.'
segments.s395-g13: The clause begins with the subordinating/quotative 'که' ('that') and a past-tense second-person form 'گفتی' ('you said') introducing reported speech: 'that you said that ...'. The nested 'که' again introduces the reported content: 'لشکر به دریا برد' — literal order 'army to sea carried' (poetic compression where 'برد' functions as 'carried/took' or 'went').
segments.s395-g14: This clause names the object 'سر خویش' ('his own head') with the enclitic possessive separated in the manuscript as خوی + ش (here treated as 'his'), followed by the object-marker 'را'. The preposition 'بر' + destination 'ثریا' ('to/against the Pleiades') and final verb 'برد' (past 'took/carried') complete the action: 'they carried his head up to the Pleiades.' Note the poetic placement of the possessive elements.
segments.s395-g15: دگر منزل اکنون sets the scene 'now, at another station/stage' (منزل = stage/station). چه بینم شگفت is an exclamatory construction where چه + verb (بینم = 'I see' in an optative/first‑person present-subjunctive form) + شگفت ('amazing/wonder') yields 'what a wonder I see' or 'how strange it is to see.'
segments.s395-g16: کز این جادو is a causal/relative clause 'that/from this magic' (کز = 'that/from which'); اندازه باید گرفت is an impersonal construction with باید ('must/ought') + گرفت (past infinitival/participial 'be taken' / 'take'), so literally 'a measure must be taken.' Together: 'Because of this sorcery, a measure/limit must be taken.'
segments.s395-g33: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). بازگردی here functions as the idea of 'returning' (a verbal noun or generic action) rather than an explicit aorist verb with a subject. بود is the past copula used in a conditional sense ('would be' / 'were'), and سودمند is the predicative adjective ('useful'). Together they form an unreal/conditional clause: 'If returning were useful...'.
segments.s395-g34: نیازی به ... is a predicative noun phrase 'a need for ...' whose copula is understood from the previous بود (the repeated verb/copula is often omitted in poetry). In context the clause is understood elliptically (commonly read as 'نیازی به ... نبود' = 'there would have been no need'). به introduces the object of need; سیمرغ و کوه بلند is a coordinated noun phrase 'the Simorgh and the high mountain'.
segments.s395-g35: از او marks the target of the emotion/action ('at him' / 'because of him'). در before the verb (در بخندید) is an adverbial/formative use found in classical Persian meaning roughly 'in/with a laugh' or an emphatic verbal formation; بخندید is 3rd-person past ('he laughed'). و گفت is the past 'and he said' (the dictionary senses given for گفت in the data do not include the verbal 'to say', so the sense index for گفت is not available). ای + شگفت is an exclamatory address 'O marvel!'.
segments.s395-g36: به پیکان marks the instrument/goal ('to/onto an arrow'); بدوزم is a 1st-person volitive/subjunctive form used like a future/intent ('I will sew/fasten'). The explicit subject من is emphatic. او را is the definite object with the accusative marker را; دو کتف 'two shoulders' specifies where he will be fastened. Word order (verb before subject) and the use of بدوزم reflect older/poetic verbal morphology.
segments.s395-g37: بِبَرَم (here written ببرم) is a 1st-person volitive/subjunctive used as 'I will cut' in classical Persian (the supplied lemma senses did not include 'to cut', so the exact sense is not present in the provided list). به + instrument (here 'به شمشیر هندی') marks the instrument ('with an Indian sword'); برش is the nominal 'cut/slice' that is being produced or driven in.
segments.s395-g38: به خاک اندر آرم: اندر is the older/prepositional 'into/within' and آرم is a 1st-person form meaning 'I place / I set' used here in a volitive/future sense ('I will lay/place into the earth'); the verb's copular/volitive use is classical. ز بالا سرش is a directional/locative phrase 'from above his head' (ز = 'from', بالا = 'above', سر + ش = 'his head'), specifying how/where the head will be laid. Note: the provided senses for آرم do not match this volitive/causative use, so its sense index is -1.
segments.s395-g39: چو introduces a temporal/simile clause ('when' / 'as'). خورشید تابنده is a noun phrase 'the shining sun' (noun + participial adjective), بنمود is the 3rd-person past 'showed/turned (its face/back)', and پشت is the noun 'back' that is shown. The clause means 'when the resplendent sun turned/showed its back...'.
segments.s395-g40: دل خاور is a genitive construction 'the heart of the East' (دلِ خاور). از پشت او = 'from behind him' (از = 'from', پشت = 'back', او = 'his'). شد is the past of شدن 'became', and درشت is a predicative adjective 'coarse/large/rough'; together they mean 'the Eastern heart became coarse/rough from behind him' (i.e. it was affected by what happened behind him). Word order places the genitive and cause before the copula and predicate, typical of poetic syntax.

sentences.s395-s49: His body grew trembling and his visage turned pale; he kept moving about, his heart full of pain.
sentences.s395-s50: The young king struck aside the inner curtain and entered the circle of brave, bright‑souled men.
sentences.s395-s51: They adorned the ground with brocade, sat at the table (banquet), and were about to begin.
sentences.s395-s13: If an elephant were to see it, he would lift a whale from the sea into the clouds and Hezbar from the land.
sentences.s395-s14: He suffers no hardship from lifting it; you size him up like a wolf and like a conjurer — judge/measure him accordingly.
sentences.s395-s15: There are two young with him on his back; the same counsel is always joined with his counsel.
sentences.s395-s16: When he rose into the air and spread his wings, the earth had no sense and the sun no splendour.
sentences.s395-s33: When the young ones saw the Simurgh, they cried out in a tumult and blood dripped from both eyes.
sentences.s395-s34: They swept them away so completely from that place that, of their portion, sight lost its way.
sentences.s395-s35: When the Simurgh was made weak by those blades, it washed chest and heavens with a stream of blood.
sentences.s395-s36: Esfandiyar came out of the chest and bellowed, with the instruments of war.
sentences.s395-s25: The sovereign rode like the wind and saw a certain mountain whose summit rose into the air.
sentences.s395-s26: He spread that shadow over horse and heaven, and entrusted his spirit to thought.
sentences.s395-s27: He kept singing praise to the One God, for the world came under His command.
sentences.s395-s28: When the Simurgh from a distance saw the chest, it saw behind it an army and the wailing of trumpets.
sentences.s395-s45: From mountain to mountain the ground was covered with feathers; because of its feathers the whole plain was full of glory.
sentences.s395-s46: They saw the king’s body, soaked with blood; “Where have you fixed your gaze upon that moon-like face?”
sentences.s395-s47: The chiefs continually cried “Bravo!” to him — the war-horsemen and the trench-diggers.
sentences.s395-s48: He heard that speech in the wolf-infested time, that the famed ruler became victorious.
sentences.s395-s29: From the mountain there came forth like a black cloud; neither the sun nor the moon shone.
sentences.s395-s30: To that evil — that the heavens might seize with a talon — in the way a leopard seizes its prey.
sentences.s395-s31: Against those swords his two feet and two wings struck; nothing of the Simurgh's beauty and royal glory remained.
sentences.s395-s32: It throbbed/struggled for a while with talons and beak; when it was drawn into a narrow place, it sank down and lay still.
sentences.s395-s41: You drove the magicians from their place; you were the guide to this good deed.
sentences.s395-s42: At that moment a cry arose from the trumpets; Peshotan brought out the stage-curtain.
sentences.s395-s43: Armament (saliḥ), the army's comrade and the son of Iran's great ones — and crown and belt.
sentences.s395-s44: From that slaughter no one saw the plain's surface; only the bodies of warriors and blood were seen.
sentences.s395-s1: The world-seeker, before the Creator, rubbed his face on the ground for a while.
sentences.s395-s2: Upon that grove, within the inner-curtain chamber he struck, and they set a banquet such as was fitting.
sentences.s395-s3: The king then commanded the torturer to bring the unfortunate one bound and humbled.
sentences.s395-s4: They brought him before the hero Esfandiyar; when the king beheld him, ...
sentences.s395-s9: Thus the wolf‑like one gave him an answer: “O war‑elephant — at times a battlefield...”
sentences.s395-s10: In this condition the task is the more difficult; be more determined and more alert.
sentences.s395-s11: You see a certain mountain with its summit in the air; upon it sits a sovereign bird.
sentences.s395-s12: Which the Simurgh calls a seeker of deeds; like a mountain‑bird, (he) is a seeker of battle.
sentences.s395-s37: Armored and with an Indian blade in its talon, what power could the bird muster against the whale?
sentences.s395-s38: The sword kept striking at it until it was torn; thus the would‑be rescuer bird became wretched.
sentences.s395-s39: He came before the Lord of the Moon, for he had given to every demon a device.
sentences.s395-s40: Thus he said, 'O just Judge, Lord of purity, strength, and skill.'
sentences.s395-s21: He raised the heads of the host's warriors and took the Simurgh's words into his mind.
sentences.s395-s22: All night he kept driving (riding) his company with him; when the shining sun rose from the mountain...
sentences.s395-s23: He renewed the lamp of time and the earth, and set a different measure upon town and plain.
sentences.s395-s24: He took that very steed and the heavens and the chest, and entrusted the army to the army's commander.
sentences.s395-s5: He gave him three goblets of kingly wine; the fierce one became merry from the ruby wine.
sentences.s395-s6: He said to him, 'O Turk, your fortune has turned; behold the old magician's head on the tree.'
sentences.s395-s7: You said that the army went to the sea; they carried off his head up to the Pleiades.
sentences.s395-s8: Now, at another station, what a wonder I see! From this magic a measure must be taken.
sentences.s395-s17: If returning were of any use, there would be no need for the Simorgh and the high mountain.
sentences.s395-s18: He laughed at him and said, 'O marvel!';
sentences.s395-s19: I will cut him with the Indian sword, drive the slice into the earth, and from above lay his head there.
sentences.s395-s20: When the shining sun turned its back, the heart of the East became coarse from behind him.
